title: Routing bath water to the garden
subtitle: A simple diverter and short pipe run to a water butt or directly to beds
excerpt: A bath or shower produces 80 to 150 litres of lightly-used warm water per use. In a metered household in summer, diverting this to the garden rather than the drain saves 1,500 to 3,000 litres per month. A basic gravity diverter and a short pipe run requires no planning permission. This covers the routing options, soap compatibility, and regulatory limits.
sourceNotes: Environment Agency greywater reuse guidance. CIRIA Report C609 Rainwater and greywater use in buildings. Water Regulations Advisory Scheme (WRAS) greywater reuse guidance.

glossaryTerms.definition: Wastewater from baths, showers, and washbasins (but not toilets or kitchen sinks). It contains soap, skin particles, and low-level organic matter but no faecal contamination.

content.text: diversion is legal in England and Wales. It must be used within 24 hours and not sprayed on edible crops. No planning permission is needed. If the property is on a combined sewer system, check the title deed or call the water company first.

Ground floor bathroom above an outside wall: a straight 40 mm waste pipe run through the outside wall to a lidded water butt is the simplest install. Drill through the wall with a 55 mm core drill, insert a 40 mm pipe with a manual 3-way diverter valve on the internal waste pipe. The diverter lets you switch between sewer (default) and butt (summer use).

First-floor bathroom: the waste pipe will need a downpipe run on the exterior wall to reach a butt or bed at ground level. The pipe must fall at a minimum 1:40 gradient throughout (2.5 cm drop per metre run) to prevent pooling.

Direct to a soakaway or mulched bed (no storage): a direct pipe from the bath to a shingle-lined soakaway or a mulched fruit tree bed avoids storage (and the bacterial growth risk). The water soaks in within minutes. This approach works only where the bed is within 5 m of the waste outlet and the ground is freely draining.

Bath water with standard shampoo, shower gel, and soap is safe for ornamental plants and established trees. The sodium from soap is absorbed by soil bacteria within days. Avoid using it on: acid-loving plants (sodium raises soil pH over time), seedlings and young transplants (soap residue can damage leaf wax), and lawns in dry periods. Never use bath water containing bleach, disinfectant, or chemical products.

Greywater stored in a sealed butt at ambient summer temperatures (15 to 25°C) begins to develop odour and bacterial growth within 24 to 48 hours. Use it within 24 hours of collection. If a butt fills during a period of rain and the water is not needed immediately, redirect the diverter back to the sewer rather than letting greywater sit. A separate 200-litre butt dedicated to greywater (not mixed with rainwater) makes it easier to manage the 24-hour window.
